entry.title: Product value help not showing any product list for a sales order

While creating a new sales order, the product list is not showing.  


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Product value help not showing any product list for a...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

 

The product list is determined by the Sales Organization and Distribution Channel. However, if the distribution channel differs between the sales order and product master data, the system will not display the product in the list. The products will only be displayed when the Sales Organization and Distribution Channel match between the sales order and product.

 

Best Regards,

entry.title: ABAP CDS data model design
entry.content:
Hi All

I am looking for some guidance on how to redesign a complex custom ABAP CDS data model which has been developed inefficiently and which is causing a lot of performance issues. The main ABAP CDS entity has a lot of joins and it takes a huge amount of HANA database time to execute the query. I am looking for some guidance to see how best to break down the one large CDS entity with multiple joins to multiple smaller CDS entities? 

Thanks Amit

entry.title: Re: Maximum Limit on Rebate | Settlement Management | Con...
entry.content:
Hi @priya_sshekhar, 

You should try with C "best condition between the 2 exclusions group". 
Best means that it will deactivate the HIGHEST amount between the 2 groups of conditions exclusion. 
Least favorable means it will deactivate the LOWEST amount between the 2 groups of conditions exclusion. 

Regards. 
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: Call SAP Digital Manufacturing Production Processes via REST API
entry.content:
Introduction

Production Processes (PPDS) within SAP Digital Manufacturing can be initiated out of the box in many different ways, including automatic triggers and POD action buttons, for example. Calling production processes from the external world via API, including from 3rd party solutions, is also a common implementation requirement.

In this blog post, I will show you how to call Production Processes via the 'Process Engine' API registered in SAP Digital Manufacturing´s Service Registry.

Implementation Steps

First, you need to Create a Service Instance and Service Key in SAP Digital Manufacturing Subaccount and get public-api-endpoint, clientid, clientsecret and url values.

Then, create, save and deploy your production process, but make sure you click "..." > 'Edit Header' in the top right corner of the PPD and switch ON 'Publish to Service Registry' option.

Then, go to Manage Service Registry app, filter, find, select your production process and get 'URL / Path' value.

Now, you can call your production processes from the external world via REST, using <url>+'/oauth/token', 'clientid' and 'clientsecret' values from the Service Key for OAUTH 2.0 client credential authentication, and 'public-api-endpoint' from the Service Key + 'URL / Path' from Manage Service Registry app as URL. See a Postman example below:

* For 'Access Token URL', use the 'url' field from service key and add '/oauth/token' to the end. A typical access token service URL, for example, should look like: https://{$Subdomain}.authentication.xxxx.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Which runs successfully and returns  "processInstanceName": "TriggerAlert#2537", which can be also seen in SAP Digital Manufacturing at Monitor Production Processes app.

You can map production process input parameters using JSON, example:

Which can be seen in Monitor Production Processes as well:

Conclusion

Using the 'Process Engine' API registered in service registry, you can trigger SAP Digital Manufacturing Production Processes from the external world via REST API.

The scenario described in this blog post is as simple as possible for educational purposes, but can be further extended and leveraged in more complex scenarios easily.

Do you like this post? Please let me know in the comments section what you think. Any feedback is highly appreciated.

Or, if you have any questions, please check SAP Community Q&A Area, or comment down below.

Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: 🇩🇪 Combine SAP Cloud Application Programming Model with SAP HANA Cloud to Create Full-Stack Applic
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event will be about Combining the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model with SAP HANA Cloud to Create Full-Stack Applications.

Join us for this day event of developer networking and hands-on learning at the Zalando Offices in Berlin, Germany.

In this CodeJam we will learn how to use an instance of SAP HANA Cloud, develop a multi-target application using SAP Business Application Studio and SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, and create a service layer and SAP Fiori UI that also includes SAP HANA native artifacts, such as calculation views.

You can make yourself familiar with the prerequisites and the learning content through the CodeJam content repository on GitHub - https://github.com/SAP-samples/cap-hana-exercises-codejam.

This free, hands-on, in-person event (not virtual) will take place on Friday, January 17th 2025, in Berlin, Germany at the Zalando offices.

The instructors for this event are the Developer Advocates @KevinR and @Vitaliy-R .

There are essential prerequisites that every attendee must complete before arriving at the event location. You can find the prerequisites here: https://github.com/SAP-samples/cap-hana-exercises-codejam/blob/main/prerequisites.md

If you wish to attend, please indicate that using the "Yes" button in the Will you be attending? box on this page.

entry.title: After submitting the change request,when i am checking in workflowlog my workitem id is blank
Re: SAP B1 profit & loss statement has recorded a cost tr...
entry.content:
Hi,

This cannot be determined with ease what has happened.

A few things may be you can check is by running the detect query in SAP Note 1448514. Another thing which can be checked is by running the following query on the SQL/HANA studio:

Select * from JDT1 where "Account" ="ShortName" and "Account" = '607901' and "Debit" = 17.56;

Select * from JDT1 where "Account" ="ShortName" and "Account" = '607901' and "Credit" = 17.56;

Check if more than one entry, then is there a different in some field value in both or not.

Kr,

Jitin

entry.title: HTTP calls to SAP Cloud Integration from API Management getting rejected - HTTP 500
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

I am trying to expose a SAP CI api via SAP API management. SAP CI Iflow has a Http Sender Adapter that accepts the payload and pushes it to a JMS Queue.

Inbound requests from postman towards the SAP CI HTTP url using the client id client secret are successful and give a 202 as expected.

When the same API is exposed via API Management it throws a Http 500 , a message log is not created in SAP CI message monitor and there is just a standard response like below.

<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">

<head>
    <title>HTTP Status 500 – Internal Server Error</title>
    <style type="text/css">
        body {
            font-family: Tahoma, Arial, sans-serif;
        }
When i tried to check the logs in SAP CI , I could see that the requests are hitting SAP CI but are getting rejected due to reasons I don't fully understand. 

 

 

SAP API Management has SAP CI as a API Provider - Internet, It's not of type Cloud Integration as I didnt have a working username password and using my suser always throws up a 401 in Test Connection.
Here is my API provider Config - URL is SAP CI Runtime url and not the tenant management url

This is how my debug trace looks. nothing complex here , just getting username password from key value map and then using Basic auth step to place it in the header.

 


  

It's not giving 401 but somehow SAP CI doesnt like the request
Can you please help me understand what the error in the trace means.

Regards

Vinay
#integrationsuite #sapci #sapapimanagement #apimanagement #api #cloudintegration

entry.title: Re: SAP PS conversion project.
entry.content: Hi, ok, then without knowing which kind of organization or Company you are working with some thoughts. Because you are doing conversion, I assume that PS-module have been used already, some cases even decades... Most probably there are customizations e.g. specific Z-reports. Have you already done fit-gap analyses (ECC/PS vs S/4HANA EPPM/CPM/PM)? What actually would be needed? Are standard (existing) features enough? Depending e.g. how much new CloudAPAB will be needed, impacts costs and time, also migrations, tests, change management, etc., etc.

entry.title: Updating the ShortText of an Inspection Lot
entry.content:
Hallo,

I am trying to update the short text of an inspection lot using SAVE_TEXT. But I can not get it to work. Is there any other way to update the short text of the Inspection Lot. Or How can I fix this approach.

Any help is appreciated.

Thanks

DATA lt_lines          TYPE TABLE OF tline.
DATA ls_line           TYPE tline.
DATA ls_header         TYPE thead.

" Prepare header
        ls_header-tdobject = 'QPRUEFLOS'.
        ls_header-tdname   = ls_usagedec-insplot. " Inspection Lot Number
        ls_header-tdid     = 'QAVE'.
        ls_header-tdspras  = sy-langu.
" Prepare text line
        ls_line-tdformat = '*'.
        ls_line-tdline   = lv_shorttext. " Text to be filled in the ShortText

CALL FUNCTION 'SAVE_TEXT'
          EXPORTING  CLIENT           = SY-MANDT
                     header           = ls_header
                     insert           = 'X'
                     savemode_direct  = 'X'
          TABLES     lines           = lt_lines
          EXCEPTIONS id              = 1
                     language        = 2
                     name            = 3
                     object          = 4
                     OTHERS          = 5.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
          " Handle error in setting text
          APPEND VALUE #( msgty      = 'E'
                          msgid      = 'ZQ'
                          msgno      = '001'
                          msgv1      = 'Error setting text for inspection lot'
                          msgv2      = ls_usagedec-insplot
                          prot_zeile = 'Error setting text for inspection lot' ) TO e_protocol.
          e_subrc = 4.
ENDIF.

CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'.

entry.title: Re: 4 Steps to getting SAP SuccessFactors Best Practices Content in your Customer Implementations
entry.content:
@sreekrithi Regarding the first recommendation - we want only experienced and certified consultants to deploy best practices content in a customer tenant. If an end-user (with access to the Upgrade Center) accidentally runs a best practices upgrade, it might change their existing configuration.

Regarding the second recommendation - we want all the configurations deployed in the customer tenant to be tested thoroughly and transported to production.

  sql: { names: 'plain', dialect: 'sqlite' },
  hana: { 'deploy-format': 'hdbtable', journal: { 'change-mode': 'alter' } },
  build: { target: 'gen' },
  mtx: {
    api: { model: true, provisioning: true, metadata: true, diagnose: true },
    domain: '__default__'
  },
  cdsc: { moduleLookupDirectories: [ 'node_modules/' ] },
  query: { limit: { max: 1000 } },
  plugins: {
    '@sap/cds-fiori': {
      impl: 'C:\\Projects\\BTP\\uisap-warehouse\\node_modules\\@sap\\cds-fiori\\cds-plugin.js',
      packageJson: 'C:\\Projects\\BTP\\uisap-warehouse\\node_modules\\@sap\\cds-fiori\\package.json'
    }
  },
  env: 'hybrid',
  '_home_cds-dk': 'C:\\Users\\<USERNAME>\\AppData\\Roaming\\npm\\node_modules\\@sap\\cds-dk'
}

entry.title: Re: KSII actual activity price calculation shows only fix...
entry.content:
Hello @路人甲-WS , how you are calculating plan prices manually via KP26 (it seems you are following KP26) or following SAP plan price calculation approach using KP06, then plan split & plan activity price calculation.

If using options 2 then it seems system will bifurcate actual activity price into Fixed & variable following below approach.

1. All cost elements for which activity dependent cost planning is done in KP06 will be used in variable activity price calculation. (linked to activity)

2. All cost element for which activity independent cost planning is done in KP06 will be used in Fixed activity price calculation.(Cost will incur irrespective of activity)

Regards,

Sumesh Gupta

entry.title: Re: Differences in SAP configuration steps required for B...
entry.content:
First, it is helpful to understand that in standard SAP ECC or SAP S/4HANA terminology, “Build-to-Order (BTO)” is not a separate “official” planning strategy number like MTO (Make-to-Order, strategy 20) or ATO (Assemble-to-Order, strategies 81/82). Instead, BTO is more of a conceptual term often realized via:

•A variant of MTO (e.g., using Make-to-Order with Variant Configuration, often strategy 25/26), or

•A specialized combination of standard MTO plus advanced Variant Configuration setups, or

•Even an ATO-like scenario where final assembly processes are heavily configured at the time of order.

 

Below is a guide to the key differentiators and typical configuration steps if you want to implement a scenario you might call “Build-to-Order” in SAP—and how they compare to MTO or ATO.

 

1. Conceptual Differences Among MTO, BTO, and ATO

1.MTO (Make-to-Order)

•Planning Strategy 20 (most common).

•Production of the entire finished good is triggered by a specific sales order.

•Stock is carried on a sales-order-specific basis (no anonymous stock).

•Usually standard BOMs/routings with no or little advanced configuration.

2.BTO (Build-to-Order)

•A type of MTO concept, but with emphasis on customization.

•Often relies on Variant Configuration so each sales order’s build can differ based on the customer’s configuration/requirements.

•Could be implemented using standard MTO strategy (20) plus advanced VC, or other MTO+VC strategies (e.g., 25/26), or even usage of certain item category groups/requirements classes that enforce “build” triggered by a sales order with configuration.

3.ATO (Assemble-to-Order)

•Planning Strategies 81/82.

•Many sub-assemblies (or semi-finished goods) are produced ahead of time (forecast-based) and stocked.

•Final assembly (the last step) is triggered by the specific sales order.

•The emphasis is on quickly assembling from pre-produced components once the order arrives.

 

2. Typical SAP Configuration Steps for a BTO Scenario

 

Since BTO is not a single strategy in the SAP standard library, you typically implement it via MTO plus Variant Configuration. Below are the key configuration areas you would set up or adjust:

1.Material Master Settings

•MRP 3 View

•Assign a Planning Strategy suitable for MTO with configuration (e.g., 20, 25, or 26) depending on your business requirements.

•Ensure the Item Category Group supports a configurable material if you are using Variant Configuration (often KMAT or 0002 for configurable materials).

•MRP 4 View

•Check “Individual/Collective Requirements” for controlling whether stock is specifically managed per sales order or collectively.

2.Requirements Type / Requirements Class

•You may need a custom requirements type (e.g., “KE**” or “KM**”) that points to a requirements class designed for BTO.

•In that requirements class, set parameters such as:

•Individual/Collective indicator = 1 (Individual) so that each customer order triggers its own planned/production order.

•Strategy Group aligned to MTO with configuration.

•Consumption Mode/Settings might be adjusted depending on how you want to consume forecasts (if at all).

3.Variant Configuration Setup (if BTO implies heavy customer-specific configuration)

•Material Classification: Assign characteristics, classes (Class type 300 for variant config).

•Configuration Profile: Indicates that the material is configurable, references the class, sets up order BOM, dependencies, etc.

•Dependencies (Constraints, Pre-Conditions, Selection Conditions, Procedures): Ensure correct BOM components and operations are selected based on the customer’s choices.

•Super BOM and Super Routing: Master data that includes all possible components and operations. Dependencies drive which components/operations are “selected” for a given configuration.

4.Sales Order Processing / Item Category Configuration

•Ensure that the Item Category for the configurable material (e.g., TAN, TAC, etc.) properly triggers the requirements type that leads to an MTO production order.

•Check that the schedule line category is set to create planned/production orders as soon as the sales order is saved (or as you require, depending on your business process).

5.Costing & Settlement

•If each sales order is to track its own production costs (commonly in a BTO scenario), configure sales order costing and set up the relevant settlement profiles so the cost flows from the production order to the sales order.

 

3. How These Steps Differentiate BTO from MTO or ATO

1.Compared to “Plain” MTO (strategy 20)

•BTO typically requires Variant Configuration and the ability to alter BOM/routings dynamically per order.

•MTO in its simplest form may just be a single-finish-product scenario with no or minimal customization. BTO is a more sophisticated approach for highly individualized products.

2.Compared to ATO (strategies 81/82)

•ATO pre-produces major sub-assemblies (forecast-based) and only does final assembly after the order arrives.

•BTO implies you are not only final-assembling after the order but often building from scratch once the order is placed—especially if the product is highly customized.

•ATO is about reducing lead time by having semi-finished stock on-hand; BTO is about customizing and building the entire product per order requirements (sometimes from raw materials upwards).

 

4. Summary

•BTO in SAP is generally a specialized Make-to-Order process involving Variant Configuration or other advanced setup steps to allow customer-specific builds.

•There is no single standard planning strategy called “BTO,” but you can realize the concept by combining standard or custom MTO strategy with the relevant requirements class, item categories, and—if needed—variant configuration.

•The main difference from plain MTO is heavier reliance on configuration and customization in the production process. The main difference from ATO is that sub-assemblies typically are not pre-built to stock—everything is triggered and “built” upon order receipt, giving a higher degree of customization but typically longer lead times.

 

By tailoring your requirements type/class, variant configuration master data, and item category/schedule line settings, you will effectively create a BTO scenario that fully differentiates it from standard MTO or ATO in SAP.

 

>>>>>FULL DISCLOSURE: This response is a test I am performing by leveraging GPT o1. If it helps you, please let me know, If it misses the mark, pls provide feedback.<<<<<

entry.title: Re: EHS Duplicate Resource Error in Identify Risk app
entry.content:
A “duplicate resource” error in the Identify Risk app typically suggests that the same “object” (risk, resource, or underlying EH&S data record) is being referenced more than once in a way the system does not allow. In other words, the app sees the same unique key or resource ID appearing in two places and throws an error when you try to open or edit one of them.

 

Below are a few common causes and suggestions for troubleshooting:

1.Check for Duplicate Risk Assignments

•In the backend (e.g., via transaction CBUH or other EH&S configuration), make sure that the same risk record is not assigned to multiple job steps or operations in a conflicting manner.

•Sometimes users accidentally create two identical entries (with the same hazard code, same substance, or same classification) under a single job step.

2.Look at Specification and Phrase Management

•If your EH&S setup includes specification data (e.g., for chemicals) or uses phrase management, verify whether two identical phrases or specs are being pulled in.

•In some cases, the same specification (e.g., “Aluminum chloride – Welding hazard”) might have been duplicated or slightly renamed, confusing the app into thinking there are two separate references.

3.Check Customizing (IMG) for Risk Types and Categories

•It’s possible that there is a configuration issue where two different “risk categories” or “risk types” are using the same underlying resource identifier.

•Ensure that each risk type/hazard category has a unique name and ID, and that those IDs are not reused across categories or controls.

4.Verify the OData Service or Front-End/Back-End Synchronization

•If you are using a Fiori app (Identify Risk) over an S/4HANA EH&S backend, check whether the OData service is returning two identical entries for the same hazard. You can use browser dev tools (Network tab) or /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE in the backend to monitor the calls.

•Sometimes stale or duplicated records in the front-end cache can trigger the error, so clearing the cache or reloading the metadata might help.

5.Look for a Known SAP Note or Patch

•SAP occasionally delivers fixes for scenarios where the Identify Risk app incorrectly flags resources as duplicates. If you are up-to-date on notes/support packs, check the SAP Support Launchpad for any KBA or SAP Note that addresses “duplicate resource” errors in S/4HANA EH&S.

•If you cannot find an exact match, log an SAP incident with details of your scenario, including screenshots and system logs.

6.Try Consolidating or Deleting One of the Entries

•If you see two apparently identical “Corrosion” hazards for the same chemical or job step, see if you can remove one entry or at least rename/modify it so it’s not exactly the same (e.g., “Corrosion – Aluminum chloride” vs. “Corrosion – Welding Byproduct” if indeed they are intended to be distinct).

•Once you ensure each hazard is truly unique, the app should no longer perceive them as duplicates.

 

In short, the key is to figure out where the duplicate is coming from: it could be specification data, hazard assignment data, or a mismatch in config. If you confirm that the two “Corrosion” hazards are truly meant to be separate, try giving them different IDs/labels; if they are duplicates, remove or merge them so only one reference remains. If all else fails, check for relevant SAP Notes or open a case with SAP Support.

 

>>>>>FULL DISCLOSURE: This response is a test I am performing by leveraging GPT o1. If it helps you, please let me know, If it misses the mark, pls provide feedback.<<<<<

entry.title: Taking Groups to the Next Level with Application-Specific Groups
entry.content:
Application-specific groups, one of the most anticipated features in SAP Cloud Identity Services, can be created in the Identity Directory by running provisioning jobs or directly via the administration console UI. Afterwards, users can be assigned to or unassigned from these groups - a step that brings us closer to the ultimate goal: user assignments or unassignments will trigger automatic group provisioning to the target systems.

What is so special about the application-specific groups?

  • They help you distinguish in the Identity Directory which group comes from which application (referred to as provisioning system in the context of Identity Provisioning). Previously, this was achieved by configuring prefixes for the groups in the respective provisioning system property. For example, setting sf.group.prefix= SF_ for SAP SuccessFactors.
  • They serve as a link between applications (consumers of Identity Authentication as identity provider) and provisioning systems (connectors to those applications) within SAP Cloud Identity Services. Each application-specific group provisioned by Identity Provisioning (IPS) contains information about the application it belongs to through the application ID.
  • They are classified into three types: User Group, Authorization and Deep Link Activation Permission.
    • User Group: Represents a collection of users based on common characteristics, such as department.
    • Authorization: Refers to application-specific objects related to permissions, such as business roles and policies.
    • Deep Link Activation Permission: Refers to a set of permissions that enable users to access specific areas of an application via URL or hyperlink. For more information, see Application-Specific Groups.

Based on your starting point, choose one of the following approaches:

 

Creating Groups (Greenfield Approach)

In this approach, you are starting from scratch. You have no applications or provisioning systems set up in the SAP Cloud Identity Services admin console, and no groups have been provisioned yet.

1. Log in to SAP Cloud Identity Services admin console.

2. Create an application. Navigate to Applications & Resources -> Applications -> Create and fill in the details in the Create Application dialog. For example:

  • For Display Name, enter SFSF
  • For Type, select SAP SuccessFactors solution.
  • For Protocol Type, select what’s appropriate.

 

 

An application ID is generated for the newly-created application.

3. Create a source system. Navigate to Identity Provisioning -> Source Systems -> Add and create a source system of the same type as the application type in step 2. For more information, see SAP SuccessFactors.

  • For Display Name, enter a name of your choice, for example ABC.
  • For Type, select SAP SuccessFactors.
  • (Optionally) Provide a description.

4. Open the Properties tab and configure the mandatory and optional properties. Add the SFSF application ID as a value of the ips.application.id property, that is:

ips.application.id= 35bda01a-2f76-47bd-97df-94444cea6f12

 

 

5. Open the Transformations tab. If the following attribute mappings for the group entity are missing in the read transformation, choose Edit, add them and save your changes:

 

     {
        "condition": "'%ips.application.id%' !== 'null'",
        "constant": "%ips.application.id%",
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['applicationId']"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['type']",
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['type']"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['supportedOperations']",
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['supportedOperations']"
      }

 

This will ensure that IPS will provision the SAP SuccessFactors groups with the given application ID, the type and supported operation. For more information, see Application-Specific Groups.

Note: Currently only SAP Advanced Financial Closing and SAP BTP Platform Members (Cloud Foundry) source systems provide such attribute mappings in their default read transformations. These mappings are gradually being implemented for other supported provisioning systems.  

6. Create the target system. Navigate to Identity Provisioning -> Target Systems -> Add and create the identity directory as a target system. For more information, see Local Identity Directory.

  • For Display Name, enter a name of your choice, for example IdDS.
  • For Type, select Local Identity Directory.
  • (Optionally) Provide a description.
  • For Source Systems, select ABC - the name of the SAP SuccessFactors source system.

7. Navigate back to your source system and run the Read Job.

8. Verify that the application-specific groups are created in the identity directory. Navigate to Users & Authorizations -> Groups.

Let’s search for a specific group that we know exists in SAP SuccessFactors, for example: 'HR Administrators'.

 

 

Notice that the application name SFSF is displayed, as it is internally mapped to the application ID of the SFSF application. Additionally, Read is returned as supported operations and User Group as the type. 

 

Creating Groups (Brownfield Approach)

In this approach, you have your applications and provisioning systems set up in the SAP Cloud Identity Services admin console, and groups have already been provisioned.

For example, you have created an application and a source system for MS Entra ID and you have provisioned the ‘Development’ group to the identity directory. The group has been created as a user group. Although it is provisioned from MS Entra ID, the admin console UI does not indicate that the group is associated with this specific system (application). As a result, the Application Name field is empty.

 

 

1. Log in to SAP Cloud Identity Services admin console.

2. Select the MS Entra ID application that you have created, and copy its application ID.

 

 

3. Select the MS Entra ID source system, open the Properties tab, choose Edit and add the property ips.application.id= 7f187cce-2f51-4efd-9bf4-9a8aabdd1c9c

4. Open the Transformations tab. If the following attribute mappings for the group entity are missing in the read transformation, choose Edit, add them and save your changes:

 

      {
        "condition": "'%ips.application.id%' !== 'null'",
        "constant": "%ips.application.id%",
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['applicationId']"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['type']",
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['type']"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['supportedOperations']",
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['supportedOperations']"
      }

 

5. Run a provisioning job from the MS Entra ID source system to the IdDS target.

After a successful provisioning, you will get the following result:

 

 

The group is updated with the application name (linked to the application ID), its respective type, the supported operation, and its sole member, Mary Wilson.

 

 

With both approaches, you now have every ingredient in place while awaiting the cherry on top: triggering the real-time provisioning of groups when user assignment changes occur.

Stay tuned!

entry.title: Hosting SAP CodeJam in Bielefeld, Germany
entry.content:
Hello Thomas,

I hope you are doing well.

We are interested in the following topics and would be happy to host one or both topics.

Regarding the dates we are completely flexible.

We can host up to 25 participants and would organize lunch and snacks. Start would be at 9 am, end would be around 5 pm.

Location would be:

  • Hotel Steigenberger Bielefelder Hof
  • Am Bahnhof 3
  • 33602 Bielefeld
  • Germany

Thank you in advance!

David

Hi 

We have a process order BOM where we add a tiny amount of liquid created in house by another department that does not use SAP. So we don't want the liquid in the BOM to create a purchase requisition or production order. We just need the value of the liquid to be added to the process order's cost. 

How is this possible? I have tried creating the material as a type NLAG, added it to BOM as type N - non-stock item. But then the process order just seems to ignore it. I cant see it in the costing analysis.

Did you consider a small BDC (executed after save_text commited with wait option?)

During standard maintenance you could use BAdI QST02_STABILOT_WA to change short text, you could look for some BAPI_INSPLOT_* which triggers this BAdI.

entry.title: Re: Creating unit test data in the RAP context
entry.content:
@MioYasutake 

Thanks very much for clarifying! I changed the code as suggested by you which works fine when adding the mock data. However, I have a consecutive problem when changing the mock data with a MODIFY ENTITIES statement. More precisely I want to add an Entity B using CREATE BY for the existing entity A and commit after that. The code looks like this now:

cds_test_environment = cl_cds_test_environment=>     cds_test_environment = cl_cds_test_environment=>create_for_multiple_cds(
                               VALUE #(
                                       ( i_for_entity = 'Z_I_ENTITY_A' )
                                       ( i_for_entity = 'Z_I_ENTITY_B')
                                     )
                             ).

DATA my_mock_data TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF Z_R_ENTITY_A.
" Fill my_mock_data with test data values
...
" Insert test data
cds_test_environment->insert_test_data( i_data = my_mock_data ).

" Add new entity
      MODIFY ENTITIES OF Z_I_ENTITY_A IN LOCAL MODE
          ENTITY ENTITY_A
          CREATE BY \_ENTITY_B
	     AUTO FILL CID
          FIELDS  ( BusinessDataField )
          WITH VALUE #( ( %tky-key_field = my_mock_data[ 1 ]-key_field %target = VALUE #( ( BusinessDataField = ‘A’ )  ) ) )
          FAILED DATA(failed_modify)
          REPORTED DATA(reported_modify)
          MAPPED DATA(mapped_modify).

“ Here failed_modify and reported_modify is empty, mapped data contains 1 entry and sy-subrc = 0. Thus COMMIT should be possible 
COMMIT ENTITIES.

 

The commit dumps now with the message:

Error analysis

The program has indicated exception "CX_CSP_ACT_INTERNAL" as the reason for the
termination:
SQL operation 'UPDATE' returned unexpected sy-subrc '4'.

Information on where terminated

The termination occurred in ABAP program or include "CL_CSP_ACT_SAVE_TO_DB=========CP", in "LIF_SINGLE_ENTITY_HANDLER~PERFORM_DB_OPERATIONS". The
main program was "SAPMSSY4".

 

From what I can see in the debugger in LIF_SINGLE_ENTITY_HANDLER~PERFORM_DB_OPERATIONS  it looks to me like a new entry for Entity B is trying to be created (and then fails).

Remarks to the code above: The MODIFY Statement is normally in the method of my code under test. I just put it in the unit test for convenience. In both cases the dump is the same. The COMMIT is intended to be in my unit test.

entry.title: MDG Cloud Ready Mode: Custom Workflow template in SAP build process automation with S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi All,

Season's Greetings!!

As we enter 2025, technology space is evolving at a rapid pace with GenAI, Agentic LLM's findings it's way in each and every enterprise level application. With Joule taking a bigger role in all SAP cloud applications, MDG has also been integrated with Joule for cloud ready mode with S4HANA 2023 FPS02. Markus discusses this in his blog Key Presentations about SAP Master Data Governance... - SAP Community.

In the blog MDG Cloud ready Mode: Integration of S/4HANA with SAP Build process automation & SAP Build work zone I talked about step-by-step details on how to set up the integration between S/4HANA on-prem to SAP BTP build process automation. In this blog we are going to explore how to set up our own workflow template in build process automation and link it with MDG process template.

INTRODUCTION:

In any MDG implementation, a big part of the design phase is to understand the workflow requirements and create custom workflows in BRF+ to cater to these requirements. In the MDG cloud ready mode also SAP has provided option to create your own Workflow templates. This has to be done in SAP build process automation.

There are however some limitations in terms of the current capabilities. A simple example is currently the control once transferred to MDC process model, can't be sent back to build process automation workflow. 

The way in which i have setup the custom workflow is little different from what is documented in the SAP help page. You can find it here.Custom Workflow and Visibility (Optional) | SAP Help Portal.

Essentially, I have not taken the standard Workflow template application in Build process automation as base, instead I have created completely different workflow.

PS: I followed the tutorial Create an API Trigger for the Process | SAP Tutorials to create the Build process automation project, so you can refer to this tutorial for step-by-step details.

MAIN CONTENT:

1. Create a blank WF project in build process automation. This workflow will be a trigger-based workflow, where trigger will be provided by the MDG process for cloud ready mode. 

2. For a trigger-based API, we need to define the input data structure of the API which is a data type. MasterDataProcess is the data type which was created with multiple fields.

3.  We have to create the process input to the API trigger; in this case I have kept only the processID as the mandatory input for the trigger.

4. Now we need to create the process artifact, which will take the Approve or Reject decision. In this case again it's only approval, rejection scenario can also be built on the build process automation side. Approve1 is the decision form 

 

 

The add quoates step, is technical step as the payload to S4HANA need to contain quotes for the WF ID instance. Currently its going without quotes, this might be overcome with future releases.

4. Once this is completed, we need to invoke a particular service SWF_CPWF_NOTIFICATION_SRV which will call S/4HANA and send the Workflow decision and ensure the process continues further. 

This service needs to be added as a dependency to your build process automation project.

This service expects SAP client and the workflow instanceID as input.

5. Once all these steps are completed the build process automation project along with the API triggers need to be deployed.

6. From the monitoring tab we can locate our custom workflow which is deployed and copy the ID.

7. Create a custom process template and in step workflow column paste the ID from step6.

Once this step is completed, the integration between the custom workflow template and the S4HANA MDG cloud ready mode process template is completed.

9. Below are the 2 methods which are useful for troubleshooting any integration issues with your custom workflow.


CONCLUSION:

This blog outlines few of the important steps, needed to deploy a custom workflow template in Build process automation and integrate it with MDG cloud ready mode process template on S4HANA on-prem.

MDG cloud ready mode is the next gen deployment option for Master data governance following the cloud first approach of SAP.

Hope you this blog provides some guidance during your MDG cloud ready mode implementation.

Please share your thoughts and questions if any.

Regards

Anirban

SAP Master Data Governance   SAP Build Process Automation   SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition   SAP S/4HANA   SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition SAP Business Technology Platform   ABAP Cloud   SAP S/4HANA business partner

entry.title: Brownfield #SAP S4HANA implementation
entry.content:
A Brownfield #SAP S/4HANA implementation, also known as a system conversion, involves migrating your existing SAP ERP system to S/4HANA. This approach retains your historical data, customizations, and configurations, making it different from a Greenfield (new implementation) approach. Here are the key steps:

1. Preparation and Assessment:

Project Kick-off and Scoping: Define the project scope, objectives, timeline, and budget. Establish clear roles and responsibilities.
System Analysis and Readiness Check: Analyze your existing SAP system to assess its readiness for conversion. This includes checking system version, Unicode compliance, add-ons, and customizations. SAP provides tools like the Readiness Check and Maintenance Planner to assist with this.
Simplification Item Check: Analyze the simplification items in S/4HANA to identify any functionalities that have changed or been removed. This helps you understand the potential impact on your business processes and customizations.
Custom Code Analysis and Remediation: Analyze your custom code to ensure compatibility with S/4HANA. This often involves adapting or rewriting custom code to comply with S/4HANA's data model and functionalities.
Hardware and Infrastructure Assessment: Assess your existing hardware and infrastructure to ensure it meets the requirements for S/4HANA. This may involve upgrading hardware or migrating to a new infrastructure.
Business Process Analysis: Analyze your existing business processes to identify areas for improvement and optimization in S/4HANA.

2. Conversion and Technical Implementation:

Sandbox Conversion: Perform a sandbox conversion to test the conversion process and identify potential issues. This allows you to simulate the conversion in a non-production environment.
System Update and Preparation: Update your existing SAP system to the required support package level and perform necessary preparations for the conversion.
Database Migration: Migrate your existing database to SAP HANA. This is a key step in the conversion process, as S/4HANA requires the HANA database.
S/4HANA Conversion: Perform the actual S/4HANA conversion using the Software Update Manager (SUM) tool. This involves upgrading the system software and migrating data to the new data model.
Post-Conversion Activities: Perform post-conversion activities, such as adjusting configurations, validating data, and testing functionalities.

3. Testing and Validation:

Unit Testing: Test individual functionalities and processes to ensure they are working correctly in S/4HANA.
Integration Testing: Test the integration between different modules and systems.
User Acceptance Testing (UAT): Involve business users in testing the system to ensure it meets their requirements.
Performance Testing: Test the system's performance under load to ensure it can handle the expected volume of transactions.


4. Training and Go-Live:

End-User Training: Train end-users on the new S/4HANA system and any changes to business processes.
Cutover Planning: Develop a detailed cutover plan to minimize downtime during the transition to the production system.
Go-Live Execution: Execute the cutover plan and switch over to the S/4HANA production system.
Post-Go-Live Support: Provide post-go-live support to address any issues that arise after the go-live.

5. Post-Implementation and Optimization:

System Monitoring and Optimization: Monitor the system's performance and stability and make necessary adjustments to optimize performance.
Continuous Improvement: Continuously evaluate the system and identify areas for improvement and optimization.


Key Considerations for Brownfield Implementations:

Custom Code Management: Managing custom code is a critical aspect of brownfield implementations. You need to analyze, adapt, and test your custom code to ensure compatibility with S/4HANA.
Data Migration Complexity: Data migration can be complex, especially with large volumes of historical data. You need to plan and execute the data migration carefully to ensure data accuracy and completeness.
Downtime Management: Minimizing downtime during the conversion is crucial. You need to plan the cutover carefully and perform thorough testing to minimize disruptions to business operations.
Change Management: Change management is essential to ensure user adoption and a smooth transition to the new system.

By following these key steps and considering the specific challenges of a brownfield implementation, you can successfully migrate your existing SAP system to S/4HANA and take advantage of its new features and functionalities.

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mickaelquesnot_sap-activity-7281226799424921600-o1eZ?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop

entry.title: Inter-company STO with SD Delivery, Billing & Logistics Invoice verification in SAP S4HANA
entry.content:
In SAP S4HANA, an intercompany Stock Transport Order (STO) with SD delivery, billing, and logistics invoice verification involves a process where goods are transferred between two company codes within the same organization. This process leverages Sales and Distribution (SD) functionality for delivery and billing, and Materials Management (MM) for procurement and invoice verification.

Process Flow:

1. STO Creation: The process begins with the creation of an STO in the receiving company code (plant). This document specifies the material to be transferred, the quantity, and the supplying plant (in a different company code).

2. Delivery Creation: Based on the STO, an outbound delivery is created in the supplying plant. This step initiates the shipping process.

3. Picking and Goods Issue: The goods are picked from the supplying plant's storage location, and a goods issue is posted, reducing the inventory in the supplying plant.

4. Goods Receipt: The receiving plant receives the goods and posts a goods receipt, increasing its inventory.

5. Billing: The supplying company code creates an intercompany billing document for the goods shipped. This document is sent to the receiving company code.

6. Invoice Verification: The receiving company code performs invoice verification to match the billing document with the goods receipt and the original STO. Once the verification is complete, the invoice is posted.

Key Configuration and Master Data:

Material Master: The material must be set up in both the supplying and receiving plants.
Vendor Master: The supplying plant must be set up as a vendor in the receiving company code.
Customer Master: The receiving plant must be set up as a customer in the supplying company code.
Shipping Data: Shipping points and other shipping-related data must be configured in the supplying plant.
Pricing Conditions: Pricing conditions for intercompany billing must be maintained.
Output Tax Determination: Tax determination procedures for intercompany transactions must be configured.

Benefits:

Automation: The process is largely automated, reducing manual effort and errors.
Transparency: The use of SD documents provides better tracking and visibility of the goods movement.
Control: Invoice verification ensures that the receiving company code only pays for goods received.

Challenges:

Configuration: Setting up the intercompany STO process with SD delivery and billing requires careful configuration.
Master Data Maintenance: Consistent master data across company codes is crucial for smooth processing.
Reconciliation: Differences in goods receipt and billing quantities may require reconciliation.

Overall, the intercompany STO process with SD delivery, billing, and logistics invoice verification in SAP S4HANA offers a robust and efficient way to manage goods transfers between company codes. However, it requires careful configuration and master data maintenance to ensure smooth processing.
